activities_projects.title: Quantitative Analysis of Inter-particle Spaces
activities_projects.objective: To explain the lack of volume expansion during dissolution using particle theory[cite: 436, 442].
steps: Measure exactly 50 mL of water and record its mass[cite: 431].
Measure 10 g of salt and dissolve it completely in the water[cite: 432].
Record the new total mass and the final volume accurately[cite: 433].
Analyze why mass is additive while volume remains nearly constant[cite: 442].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Demonstrates that particles of salt occupy specific voids between water particles without displacing the bulk volume[cite: 442].
activities_projects.evaluation: Calculate the theoretical vs. observed density of the solution (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Microscopic Scale Estimation through Serial Dilution
activities_projects.objective: To mathematically estimate the extreme smallness of particles beyond human imagination[cite: 476, 479].
steps: Perform a 1:10 serial dilution five times as per Activity 1.2[cite: 450, 451].
Observe the threshold where the color is barely visible to the naked eye[cite: 474].
Calculate the dilution factor (e.g., $10^{-5}$) of the final solution[cite: 452].
Infer the minimum number of particles originally present in one crystal[cite: 476].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Concludes that matter is particulate and particles are infinitesimally small[cite: 476, 479].
activities_projects.evaluation: Derive a mathematical estimate of particles in the original crystal based on the dilution limit (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Kinetic Energy and Diffusion Dynamics
activities_projects.objective: To correlate temperature increases with increased kinetic energy and diffusion rates[cite: 504, 508].
steps: Record temperatures of both hot and cold water[cite: 493].
Add crystals simultaneously and measure the distance color travels per minute without stirring[cite: 494, 497].
Analyze the relationship between thermal energy and the speed of particles[cite: 503, 504].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Establishes that heating provides energy to overcome inter-particle attractions faster[cite: 504, 508].
activities_projects.evaluation: Graph the rate of diffusion against temperature (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Force Variation and State Rigidity
activities_projects.objective: To investigate how the strength of inter-particle attraction determines the physical state and rigidity[cite: 533, 563].
steps: Apply measured force to break each substance[cite: 517].
Classify substances by their resistance to shape change (rigidity)[cite: 563].
Compare these to liquids, which have no fixed shape due to weaker forces[cite: 585, 596].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Identifies that force of attraction varies significantly across different types of matter[cite: 533].
activities_projects.evaluation: Scientific report on why solids are rigid while liquids are fluids based on force strength (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Compression Gradient in Three States
activities_projects.objective: To determine the relationship between inter-particle distance and compressibility[cite: 608, 616].
materials: Three 100 mL syringes with rubber corks [cite: 603]
steps: Apply varying weights to the pistons of syringes filled with solid, liquid, and gas[cite: 607].
Measure the volume change for each increment of weight.
Observe the high speed and random movement of gas particles allowing high compression[cite: 616, 617].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Proves that gases have maximum space between particles, allowing for high-pressure storage like LPG[cite: 608, 609].
activities_projects.evaluation: Calculate the compression percentage for each state (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Latent Heat and Phase Change Analysis
activities_projects.objective: To analyze why temperature remains constant during phase transitions despite heat application[cite: 696, 701].
steps: Heat ice and record temperature every 30 seconds until 5 minutes after boiling starts[cite: 682, 686].
Observe the 'hidden' energy (latent heat) used to break inter-particle bonds[cite: 701, 711].
Compare the energy levels of steam vs. water at 373 K[cite: 710].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Understands latent heat of fusion and vaporisation as energy used for changing state, not temperature[cite: 702, 709].
activities_projects.evaluation: Construct a phase change diagram (Heating Curve) and label the latent heat plateaus (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Direct Phase Transitions: Sublimation and Deposition
activities_projects.objective: To observe state changes occurring without a liquid intermediary[cite: 724, 743].
materials: China dish, Inverted funnel, Cotton plug [cite: 728, 729, 731]
steps: Setup the sublimation apparatus as shown in Fig. 1.7[cite: 741].
Heat slowly and observe the formation of vapors (sublimation)[cite: 742, 743].
Observe the solid deposit on the cool inner walls of the funnel (deposition)[cite: 743].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Identifies substances that bypass the liquid state based on pressure and temperature[cite: 743, 750].
activities_projects.evaluation: Diagrammatic comparison of the particles in the solid and gaseous phases during this process (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Pressure-Induced Liquefaction
activities_projects.objective: To demonstrate how increasing pressure and decreasing temperature can liquefy gases[cite: 734, 746].
steps: Compress air in a syringe while keeping it in a cold bath[cite: 734, 746].
Research the industrial conversion of $CO_{2}$ to dry ice through pressure decrease[cite: 747, 748].
Analyze how state is determined by both pressure and temperature[cite: 750].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Explains how the distance between particles can be forced to change through external pressure[cite: 745, 757].
activities_projects.evaluation: Explain the logic behind naming solid $CO_{2}$ 'dry ice' (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Evaporation Kinetics and Surface Phenomena
activities_projects.objective: To distinguish evaporation from boiling as a surface phenomenon[cite: 789, 851].
steps: Measure the evaporation rate in different humidities and wind speeds[cite: 807, 810].
Compare water loss in a test tube vs. an open china dish[cite: 794, 795].
Explain how high kinetic energy surface particles break free from attraction[cite: 788].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Concludes that evaporation rate depends on surface area, temperature, wind speed, and humidity[cite: 853].
activities_projects.evaluation: Develop a multi-variable equation to represent the factors affecting evaporation (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Thermodynamics of Evaporative Cooling
activities_projects.objective: To explain the cooling effect through the absorption of latent heat from surroundings[cite: 814, 825].
steps: Measure the temperature drop on a surface after applying acetone[cite: 818, 819].
Analyze why cotton is a better absorber for sweat-induced cooling in summer[cite: 826].
Observe water droplets on an ice-cold glass to understand energy loss from water vapor in air[cite: 827, 829].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Relates the sensation of 'cold' to the loss of energy to the evaporating liquid[cite: 815, 819].
activities_projects.evaluation: Explain why a desert cooler is more effective on a hot, dry day vs. a rainy day (10 marks).
